projects.title: Green Building Certifications and Property Values in San Francisco
projects.description: An empirical analysis of whether LEED and Energy Star certifications raise commercial property values in San Francisco, using 18 years of public assessor data (2007–2024) and three identification strategies: hedonic OLS, a two-way fixed-effects difference-in-differences panel, and a renovation-matched design that isolates the certification signal from concurrent capital investment.
questions: Do LEED and Energy Star certifications produce a measurable premium in assessed improvement value after controlling for building characteristics?
Does the premium survive a renovation-matched comparison — certified+renovated vs. renovated-but-uncertified buildings?
Does the premium materialize after certification rather than after renovation alone, as confirmed by a permit-anchored event study?
Does the effect vary by property use type — offices, hotels, retail, mixed-use?
findings: Hedonic cross-section: 52% premium (β = 0.419, p < 0.001) in the 2024–25 assessor roll
DiD panel: 35% increase post-certification relative to matched controls (β = 0.302, p < 0.001)
Renovation-matched hedonic: 31% premium (β = 0.271, p = 0.033) — survives the primary identification test
Permit-anchored event study: pre-permit trends parallel; premium grows from ~year +4, peaking at +7, consistent with the 7-year average permit-to-certification gap
Heterogeneity: strongest for office (39%) and hotel (250%, small N); retail and industrial insignificant

tools.name: Python
tools.description: Primary analysis language. Used for data cleaning, merging assessor/certification/permit datasets, CEM matching, OLS and panel regressions, and all figures.

tools.name: Excel
tools.description: Preliminary data exploration and summary statistics.

resources.label: SF Assessor Secured Property Tax Roll (DataSF)
resources.description: 18 annual roll files, 2007–2024 — primary outcome variable source

resources.label: USGBC LEED Project Directory
resources.description: LEED certification registry used to identify treated parcels

resources.label: Eichholtz, Kok & Quigley (2010) — AER
resources.description: Baseline hedonic study: 3% asking rent, 16% sale price premium for certified offices

resources.label: Dermisi (2009) — Journal of Sustainable Real Estate
resources.description: Assessor-value approach most directly comparable to this paper
